review_text: Absolutely incredible dining experience! The pasta carbonara was creamy perfection - not too heavy, with perfectly crispy guanciale. Our server Antonio was attentive without being intrusive. The wine pairing recommendation was spot on. The ambiance with the exposed brick walls and warm lighting made it feel like a little corner of Rome. We'll definitely be back for our anniversary.

Food was decent but the wait was ridiculous. We had a reservation for 7 PM and weren't seated until 7:45. No apology or explanation from the host. Once seated, the bruschetta appetizer was good and the margherita pizza was above average. But the tiramisu was clearly from a mix - not house-made as the menu claims. Service was rushed once we were seated, probably because they were behind schedule.

Charming Italian restaurant with authentic flavors. The osso buco was fall-off-the-bone tender and the gremolata added a perfect brightness. The bread basket with house-made focaccia was a lovely start. Only knocking off one star because the noise level was quite high - we had to lean across the table to hear each other. Would recommend requesting the patio on nice evenings.

Food poisoning. My wife and I both got sick after eating the seafood risotto. I called the restaurant and the manager was dismissive, suggesting we must have eaten something else. We hadn't. I've reported this to the health department. This is not a matter of taste preferences - this is a food safety issue. Avoid the seafood here.

Best Italian food outside of Italy! The homemade pappardelle with wild boar ragu was extraordinary. You can taste the love and care in every dish. The chef came out to greet tables personally, which was a wonderful touch. The cannoli for dessert was crisp and fresh. Portions are generous and the price point is very reasonable for the quality. A true gem in this neighborhood.

Solid neighborhood Italian spot. Nothing groundbreaking but everything is well-executed. The caprese salad used beautiful heirloom tomatoes and fresh mozzarella. The chicken parmigiana was crispy outside and juicy inside. House Chianti was surprisingly good for the price. The only negative was the cramped table spacing - I could practically eat off my neighbor's plate.

Overpriced for what you get. The "truffle" pasta tasted like it was made with truffle oil from a bottle rather than actual truffles. The portion of the veal piccata was tiny - maybe 4 ounces of meat for $38. The bread was stale. The one saving grace was the espresso, which was genuinely excellent. But I can get great espresso without paying $150 for two people.

We hosted our engagement party here and the experience was flawless. The private dining room was beautifully decorated. The fixed menu they created for us featured seasonal dishes that showcased the chef's creativity. Every guest complimented the food, especially the lobster ravioli and the panna cotta. The staff treated us like family. Cannot recommend highly enough for special occasions.

Average Italian restaurant with above-average prices. The pizza oven produces a nice char on the crust, but the toppings were sparse. The Caesar salad was drowning in dressing. The arancini appetizer was the standout - crispy, cheesy, and delicious. Service was friendly but slow. It took 25 minutes for our entrees after the appetizer was cleared. Not bad, not great.

Great atmosphere for date night. The lighting is dim and romantic, the music is authentic Italian (not cheesy Dean Martin), and the wine list is thoughtful. My partner's eggplant parmigiana was excellent. My lamb shank was well-seasoned but slightly overcooked. The tiramisu was the best I've had outside of Florence. Definitely recommend making a reservation - it gets packed on weekends.

Third visit and it just keeps getting better. This time we tried the tasting menu and it was worth every penny. Seven courses that took us on a journey through Italian cuisine - from a delicate crudo to a rich osso buco to a refreshing lemon sorbet. The sommelier's wine pairings were educational and delicious. Three hours of pure culinary joy.

Worst service I've ever experienced. Our waiter forgot our drink order twice, brought the wrong entree to our table, and then argued with us when we pointed out the mistake. The manager apologized but didn't offer any compensation. The food that eventually arrived was lukewarm. For a restaurant that positions itself as "fine dining," this level of service is unacceptable.

Lovely family dinner spot. They accommodate children without making you feel unwelcome - kids menu, crayons, and a patient staff. The adult food is genuinely good too. My husband's spaghetti alle vongole had perfectly cooked clams. My chicken marsala was comforting and flavorful. The garlic bread is addictive. Prices are fair. We come here at least twice a month.

The food is good but the reservation system is terrible. I booked online three days in advance and when we arrived, they had no record of it. The host squeezed us into a tiny table near the kitchen, which was noisy and hot. The gnocchi was light and pillowy - clearly handmade. The bolognese was rich and meaty. But the seating experience soured the evening.

Hidden gem! We stumbled upon this place while walking through the neighborhood and I'm so glad we did. The fresh pasta is made in-house daily - you can see it through the window to the kitchen. The cacio e pepe was the simplest and most perfect version I've ever tasted. Just pasta, pecorino, and pepper, executed flawlessly. The house limoncello to finish was divine.

I have celiac disease and was told they could accommodate gluten-free requests. The waiter seemed knowledgeable, but my "gluten-free" pasta clearly wasn't - the texture was all wrong and I got sick afterward. Cross-contamination at minimum, straight-up incorrect pasta at worst. If you can't safely accommodate dietary restrictions, please be honest about it rather than putting customers' health at risk.

Beautiful restaurant with consistently good food. The antipasto platter for two was generous and varied - prosciutto, marinated vegetables, fresh mozzarella, and bruschetta. The lasagna was layered with care and had a perfect ratio of meat, cheese, and sauce. Dessert felt like an afterthought though - the gelato selection was limited and the flavors were basic. Still a solid 4 stars.

As an Italian-American who grew up eating his grandmother's cooking, I'm very particular about Italian food. This restaurant passes the test. The Sunday gravy (sauce) simmers for hours - you can taste the depth. The meatballs are tender and seasoned perfectly. The ricotta is house-made. It tastes like home, which is the highest compliment I can give an Italian restaurant.

Nice enough for a casual dinner but don't expect anything memorable. The minestrone soup was warming and hearty. The chicken piccata was well-prepared but unremarkable. The wine by the glass options were limited - only two reds and two whites. The decor is pleasant in a generic Italian restaurant way. It fills a niche in the neighborhood but won't be a destination dining spot.

Came for lunch on a weekday and was pleasantly surprised. The lunch specials offer great value - soup or salad plus an entree for under $20. My chicken Caesar wrap was fresh and well-seasoned. The tomato bisque was velvety and rich. The service was quick and efficient, perfect for a business lunch. The espresso was strong and authentic. Will add this to my lunch rotation.

We've tried every Italian restaurant within 20 miles and this is definitively the best. The fresh pasta options change weekly based on what's seasonal. Last visit, the wild mushroom pappardelle with truffle cream was transcendent. The wine list leans toward smaller Italian producers, which I appreciate. The staff's knowledge of Italian wine regions is impressive. Worth the 30-minute drive.

The quality has declined noticeably since they changed chefs six months ago. The dishes that used to be exceptional are now merely adequate. The signature ragu that drew us back repeatedly now tastes like it comes from a jar. The portions have shrunk while prices have increased. It's sad to watch a once-great restaurant coast on its reputation. We won't be returning.

Authentic Italian dining in an unpretentious setting. The wood-fired pizza is excellent - thin, crispy crust with quality toppings. The diavola with spicy salami and honey was an unexpected flavor combination that worked beautifully. The house red wine was drinkable and affordable. The only downside is the no-reservation policy on weekends - we waited 40 minutes for a table.

Celebrated my 60th birthday here and it was perfect. The staff made me feel special with a complimentary prosecco toast and a beautiful dessert plate with "Happy Birthday" written in chocolate. The food was outstanding - the seafood linguine had generous portions of shrimp, mussels, and calamari in a light white wine sauce. The atmosphere was warm and festive. Memories that will last a lifetime.

Mixed bag. The appetizers were excellent - the burrata with roasted tomatoes was creamy and fresh, and the fried calamari was perfectly tender. But the main courses were disappointing. My osso buco was tough and underseasoned. My partner's risotto was overcooked and gluey. It's like two different kitchens are preparing the starters and mains. Stick to the appetizers and pizza.

Cozy and reliable. We come here whenever we want a comforting Italian meal without fuss. The menu hasn't changed much in the two years we've been coming, but that's fine because the classics are done well. The fettuccine alfredo is rich and indulgent. The tiramisu is consistently good. The staff remembers our names, which makes us feel like regulars. Warm hospitality.

Found a hair in my risotto. When I brought it to the waiter's attention, he took the dish away and returned with the SAME dish - I could tell because I had made a distinctive mark in the rice before sending it back. When I confronted him, he denied it and the manager backed him up. Absolutely disgusting and dishonest. One star is generous.

The cooking class they offer on Saturday mornings is worth every penny. Chef Marco teaches you to make fresh pasta from scratch, along with a sauce and dessert. You eat what you make at the end. The small class size (max 8) means personal attention. I learned techniques I now use at home regularly. The restaurant experience is great too, but the cooking class is a unique offering.

Reliable Italian restaurant that rarely disappoints. The wood-fired oven produces consistently excellent pizzas. The margherita is simple and perfect - San Marzano tomatoes, fresh mozzarella, and basil. The calzone is massive and stuffed generously. The salads are fresh and well-dressed. Service is efficient. Not the most inventive Italian food, but perhaps the most consistent. And consistency matters.

The restaurant has a charming exterior and nice interior, but the food doesn't quite live up to the setting. The bruschetta was good but the tomatoes weren't ripe enough. The chicken cacciatore was competently made but lacked the depth of flavor I expect from a dish that should simmer for hours. The panna cotta was the highlight - silky smooth with a bright berry compote.

Everything about this place is authentic. The owner imports olive oil, San Marzano tomatoes, and cheese directly from Italy. You can taste the difference. The simple dishes shine brightest - a plate of hand-cut tagliatelle with just butter, Parmigiano, and black pepper was one of the most satisfying meals I've had this year. They let the ingredients speak for themselves. Bravo.

Disappointing experience for the price point. The "fresh" fish special tasted anything but fresh - it had that telltale fishy smell that indicates it's been sitting too long. The pasta was overcooked to the point of mushiness. The only positive was the bread service - warm, crusty, with excellent olive oil for dipping. But you can't build a dinner around bread alone.

Great spot for a casual weeknight dinner. The menu has enough variety to keep things interesting without being overwhelming. The sausage and pepper rigatoni is my go-to - spicy, savory, and satisfying. My wife loves the eggplant rollatini. The house wine is surprisingly good for the price. Kid-friendly without being a family restaurant. We come here at least once a month.

I'm a food critic and I try to remain objective, but this restaurant makes it difficult. The technical skill in the kitchen is evident in every dish. The risotto achieves that perfect al'onda consistency. The pasta is cooked to precise al dente. The seasoning is balanced and layered. But beyond technique, there's soul in this food. You can feel the passion. This is cooking at its finest.

Decent Italian food in a neighborhood that needed it. Before this place opened, the closest Italian restaurant was 20 minutes away. So I'm grateful it exists. The pizza is good, the pasta is acceptable, and the salads are fresh. The prices are fair. But if you've been to truly great Italian restaurants, this one won't wow you. It's a solid neighborhood joint, nothing more.

The seasonal menu changes keep things interesting. This winter, the butternut squash ravioli with brown butter and sage was heavenly. The braised short rib pappardelle was rich and warming. They pair their seasonal dishes with rotating Italian wines that complement the flavors. The staff is knowledgeable about the seasonal offerings and makes good recommendations. Always something new to try.

The worst Italian restaurant I've been to in this city. The "homemade" pasta was clearly dried pasta from a box. The "fresh" mozzarella was the pre-shredded kind from a bag. The red sauce tasted like it came straight from a Costco jar. At these prices, I expect ingredients that are at least somewhat fresh and preparation that shows some skill. This is cafeteria food at restaurant prices.

Perfect neighborhood restaurant. We live two blocks away and eat here weekly. The staff knows us and always has our preferred table ready. The consistency is remarkable - whether it's Tuesday or Saturday, the food quality never drops. The linguine with clam sauce is my comfort food. The espresso and biscotti after dinner is the perfect ending. This restaurant is part of our family's routine.

Good food and great value during happy hour (4-6 PM). Half-price appetizers and $8 glasses of wine make this an affordable option for after-work socializing. The bruschetta, arancini, and calamari are all excellent at any price, and at half price they're a steal. The full dinner menu is also fairly priced. The outdoor seating area is lovely in warm weather.

The restaurant has potential but needs to address some basics. The food ranges from good (pizza, pasta) to mediocre (fish, meat). The service is friendly but disorganized - orders get mixed up frequently. The wine list is decent but markup is steep - bottles I buy for $15 are listed at $55. The atmosphere is pleasant and the location is convenient. With some improvements, this could be a great spot.

We held our rehearsal dinner here and it exceeded all expectations. The private room was elegant, the custom menu showcased the chef's range, and the wine service was impeccable. Every course was timed perfectly - no long waits between dishes. The staff went out of their way to accommodate our dietary restrictions without making anyone feel like a burden. Truly exceptional hospitality.

A dependable Italian restaurant that I recommend to friends without hesitation. The menu covers all the classics and does them justice. The lasagna is hearty and cheesy. The chicken marsala is tender and flavorful. The cannoli are crisp and freshly filled. Nothing will reinvent Italian cuisine, but everything is prepared with care and served with warmth. That's exactly what I want from a neighborhood Italian spot.

I understand that restaurants have off nights, but this was beyond that. Forty-five minute wait despite having a reservation. Cold bread. Overcooked steak. Watery sauce on the pasta. Distracted waiter who had to be flagged down for refills. And to top it off, they automatically added an 18% gratuity to our bill for a party of two. We were not informed of this policy. Very disappointing.

The homemade limoncello is reason enough to visit. But beyond that, this is a restaurant that understands Italian cooking at its core - simplicity, quality ingredients, and proper technique. The spaghetti aglio e olio had just four ingredients and was spectacular. The branzino was perfectly grilled with just lemon and herbs. Sometimes the best cooking is knowing what NOT to add.

Solid three stars. The pizza is above average with a nice charred crust and quality mozzarella. The pasta dishes are competent but unremarkable. The desserts are clearly not made in-house. The wine list is adequate. The service is professional. The ambiance is pleasant. Everything is fine. Nothing is exceptional. If you want reliable Italian food without surprises, this delivers exactly that.

The brunch menu is a hidden gem. Italian-style brunch with dishes like eggs in purgatory, prosciutto and melon, ricotta pancakes, and Aperol spritzes. It's a nice departure from the typical eggs-and-bacon brunch spots. The portions are generous and the prices are reasonable for brunch. The weekend atmosphere is lively without being overwhelming. Definitely recommend trying brunch here.

Ten visits in and I'm still discovering things I love about this restaurant. Latest discovery: they do a Sunday supper family-style meal that's incredible value. For $45 per person, you get antipasto, two pastas, a main course, and dessert - all served family-style at long communal tables. The food is abundant and excellent. The communal dining creates a wonderful social atmosphere. Italian dining as it should be.

I made a reservation for my mother's 70th birthday and specifically requested the corner booth. When we arrived, not only was the booth given to someone else, but the host was unapologetic. We were seated at a wobbly two-top near the restrooms. The food was fine - the chicken parm was crispy and the pasta al pomodoro was fresh - but the poor handling of our reservation ruined what should have been a special evening. Communication and follow-through matter.

Stumbled in on a rainy Tuesday and had one of those unexpectedly perfect meals. The place was nearly empty so we got the chef's full attention. He sent out a complimentary amuse-bouche of bruschetta with nduja that was incredible. The veal saltimbocca was tender and perfectly seasoned. The house-made limoncello cake for dessert was light and citrusy. Sometimes the best dining experiences are the unplanned ones.

The restaurant tries hard but doesn't quite deliver on its promise of "authentic Italian." The carbonara had cream in it (real carbonara doesn't). The "fresh" basil on the pizza was clearly wilted. The espresso was good though - they use a proper Italian machine and beans. The outdoor patio is lovely for people-watching. It's a pleasant enough experience if you don't set your expectations too high.
